1: ben adam (ben ʾādām): 'son of man / son of Adam.' The most characteristic divine address to Ezekiel, used 93 times in the book (more than any other OT book). Ben ʾādām means simply 'human being' — it is the term that marks Ezekiel's creatureliness before the divine presence. The contrast is deliberate: the divine being enthroned in chapter 1 has the demut kemarat adam (likeness like a human) while the prophet is addressed as ben ʾādām (a human being) — the human below and the anthropomorphic divine above, both using the vocabulary of human form. In Daniel 7:13 the phrase becomes a messianic title ('one like a son of man'); in the NT, Jesus's consistent self-designation 'the Son of Man' draws on both traditions.
2: ruach baah vi (rûaḥ bāʾâ bî): 'the Spirit came into me.' The Spirit that animates the chariot-wheels (Ezekiel 1:20-21) is the same Spirit that now enters the prophet. The entry of the ruach into Ezekiel is the link between the visionary encounter and the prophetic commission: he cannot stand without the Spirit entering him (cf. Ezekiel 3:24). The divine voice speaks first, then the Spirit enters to enable response — the order of grace: word, then enablement to hear it.
3: Sent to the children of Israel, to the rebellious nations (goyim hamordim, gôyîm hāmōrĕdîm). The term marad (mārad) — to rebel — is stronger than the more common pāšaʿ (transgress); it carries the connotation of open political revolt against a sovereign. Israel is addressed as goyim (nations/Gentiles) — a shocking term, usually reserved for foreign peoples. Their rebellion has characterized them 'to this day' (ʿad hayyôm hazzeh): persistent, ongoing, unreformed. The fathers rebelled and the sons have not stopped.
4: Israel is azei panim (ʿazzê p̄ānîm): 'hard of face/impudent' and hazaq lev (ḥĕzaq lēb): 'hardened of heart.' The two body-part metaphors describe spiritual resistance: the face (what one presents to others) is hard/impudent — unashamed, uncowed; the heart (decision-making center) is hardened against the word. Paul's description of the unregenerate heart in Romans 1:21 (their foolish hearts were darkened) and the hardening of Israel in Romans 11:7 both draw on this OT vocabulary of ḥĕzaq lēb.
5: 'Whether they listen or not' (yishmehu veIM yichdalu, yišmĕʿû wĕʾim yeḥdālû): the prophetic mission is not conditioned on success. The prophet is called to speak the divine word regardless of reception — a theme that will be intensified in Ezekiel 3. This is the hermeneutic key to Ezekiel's entire ministry: the word goes forth whether it is received or rejected; the prophet's faithfulness is the only variable under his control. ki beit meri hemah (kî bêt mĕrî hēmmâ): 'for they are a house of rebellion' — the label beit meri (house of rebellion) recurs throughout Ezekiel as the characteristic designation of his audience.
6: 'Do not be afraid of them, do not be afraid of their words' — even when briers and thorns are around you and you sit on scorpions. The three images of the hostile audience: saravim (sārābbîm, thorns/briers), qotsim (qôṣîm, thorns/spines), aqravim (ʿaqrabbîm, scorpions). The scorpion image is particularly striking: not just passive thorns but actively dangerous creatures. The prophet is called to sit among them without flinching — divine fearlessness as the mark of genuine prophetic calling (cf. Jeremiah 1:17; 1 Kings 18:15).
7: Whether they listen or refuse — they are a rebellious house (beit meri). The repetition from Ezekiel 2:5 forms a frame around Ezekiel 2:5-7: the commission is unchanged by the audience's likely rejection. The Hebrew perfect yishmehu (they will listen) and imperfect yichdalu (they will refuse) are both uncertain futures — the prophet cannot know which will occur. But the commission stands either way.
8: 'But you, son of man, hear what I say to you — do not be rebellious like that rebellious house; open your mouth and eat what I give you.' The contrast between Ezekiel and Israel: Israel is the beit meri; Ezekiel must be the opposite. The command lo tehieh meri (lōʾ tihyê mĕrî): 'do not be a rebel' — explicitly applying the label they bear to the possible failure mode of the prophet himself. The prophet is not immune to the temptation of rebellion; the command is necessary because the commission is costly.
9: A hand was stretched out to me, and in it was a scroll (meghillat sefer, mĕgillat sēp̄er). Meghillah is a rolled scroll (from gālal, to roll). The scroll is stretched toward Ezekiel by a hand — presumably the hand of the enthroned figure of chapter 1 (whose hand of judgment/commission recurs throughout). The scroll contains the divine message that Ezekiel must internalize before proclamation.
10: Written on its front and back — panim veahor (pānîm wĕʾāḥôr): front and back, both sides. The contents: qinot vehageh vehi (qinôt wahegeh wāhî): 'lamentations and mourning and woe.' Three words for grief, all expressing the negative content of Ezekiel's prophetic message. The double-sided writing means the message is completely full — there is no space for anything else. The divine word to Judah is entirely lament. John's little scroll in Revelation 10:8-10 is the same meghillah tradition: the scroll eaten by the prophet contains judgment that is sweet to receive but bitter in effect.
